title: श्री त्रिपुरान्तक स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
त्रिपुरान्तकमीशानं देवदेवं त्रिलोचनम्।
त्रिशूलधारिणं शम्भुं नमामि परमेश्वरम्॥१॥

यस्य बाणाग्निना दग्धाः त्रिपुरास्ते पुरा सुराः।
तं त्रिपुरघ्नं देवेशं प्रणमामि सदाशिवम्॥२॥

मेरुं धनुः कृत्वा विष्णुं शरं च वासुकिं गुणम्।
चतुर्वेदान् हयान् कृत्वा रथं च पृथिवीं प्रभुः॥३॥

ब्रह्माणं सारथिं कृत्वा चन्द्रसूर्यौ रथाङ्गकौ।
त्रिपुरं दहतां देवं वन्दे त्रिपुरसूदनम्॥४॥

एकेन बाणमात्रेण यो जघान पुरत्रयम्।
तस्मै नमो भगवते त्रिपुरान्तकमूर्तये॥५॥

असुरैर्निर्मितं दुर्गं त्रिपुरं लोकपीडनम्।
क्षणेन भस्म कृतवान् नमस्तस्मै पिनाकिने॥६॥

देवानां प्रार्थनां श्रुत्वा ब्रह्मविष्ण्विन्द्रसंस्तुतम्।
त्रिपुरं दहतां शम्भुं कालाग्निरुद्रमीश्वरम्॥७॥

यो देवैरपि सम्पूज्यो ब्रह्मादिभिरनिन्दितः।
त्रिपुरान्तकरूपेण शिवाय प्रणमाम्यहम्॥८॥

त्रिपुरान्तकमाहात्म्यं यः पठेत् सततं नरः।
त्रिविधं तापमुक्तः सन् शिवलोकमवाप्नुयात्॥

॥ इति श्री त्रिपुरान्तक स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
Tripurantakam Ishanam Devadevam Trilochanam.
Trishula-Dharinam Shambhum Namami Parameshvaram.||1||

Yasya Banagnina Dagdhah Tripuraste Pura Surah.
Tam Tripuraghnam Devesham Pranamami Sadashivam.||2||

Merum Dhanuh Kritva Vishnum Sharam Cha Vasukim Gunam.
Chaturvedan Hayan Kritva Ratham Cha Prithivim Prabhuh.||3||

Brahmanam Sarathim Kritva Chandrasuryau Rathangakau.
Tripuram Dahatam Devam Vande Tripurasooanam.||4||

Ekena Banamaatrena Yo Jaghana Puratrayam.
Tasmai Namo Bhagavate Tripurantakamurtaye.||5||

Asurair Nirmitam Durgam Tripuram Lokapidanam.
Kshanena Bhasma Kritavan Namastasmai Pinakine.||6||

Devanam Prarthanam Shrutva Brahma-Vishnu-Indra-Samstutam.
Tripuram Dahatam Shambhum Kalagnirudram Ishvaram.||7||

Yo Devairapi Sampujyo Brahmadibhir Aninditah.
Tripurantakarupena Shivaya Pranamamyaham.||8||

Tripurantaka-Mahatmyam Yah Pathet Satatam Narah.
Trividham Tapamuk-tah San Shivalokam Avapnuyat.

|| Iti Shri Tripurantaka Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
त्रिपुरान्तक ईशान, देवों के देव, त्रिलोचन, त्रिशूलधारी शम्भु — परमेश्वर को मैं नमन करता हूँ।

जिनके बाण की अग्नि से पूर्वकाल में तीनों पुर भस्म हुए, उन त्रिपुरघ्न देवेश सदाशिव को मैं प्रणाम करता हूँ।

मेरु पर्वत को धनुष बनाकर, विष्णु को बाण, वासुकि को प्रत्यञ्चा, चारों वेदों को अश्व और पृथ्वी को रथ बनाकर।

ब्रह्मा को सारथि, चन्द्र-सूर्य को रथ-चक्र बनाकर — त्रिपुर को दग्ध करने वाले त्रिपुरसूदन देव की मैं वन्दना करता हूँ।

केवल एक बाण मात्र से जिन्होंने तीनों पुरों को नष्ट किया, उन भगवान त्रिपुरान्तक मूर्ति को नमस्कार।

असुरों द्वारा निर्मित दुर्गम त्रिपुर जो लोकों को पीड़ित कर रहा था, उसे क्षणभर में भस्म किया — ऐसे पिनाकधारी को नमस्कार।

देवताओं की प्रार्थना सुनकर, ब्रह्मा-विष्णु-इन्द्र द्वारा स्तुत, त्रिपुर दग्ध करने वाले कालाग्निरुद्र ईश्वर शम्भु।

जो देवताओं और ब्रह्मा आदि से भी पूज्य, अनिन्दित हैं — उन त्रिपुरान्तक रूप शिव को मैं प्रणाम करता हूँ।

जो मनुष्य त्रिपुरान्तक माहात्म्य का सतत पाठ करता है, वह तीनों तापों से मुक्त होकर शिवलोक प्राप्त करता है।

viniyog.shloka: अस्य श्रीत्रिपुरान्तकस्तोत्रमन्त्रस्य नारद ऋषिः। अनुष्टुप् छन्दः। श्रीत्रिपुरान्तकशिव देवता। ॐ नमः शिवाय बीजम्। त्रिपुरदहनशक्तिः। त्रितापनाशः कीलकम्। श्रीशिवप्रीत्यर्थे पाठे विनियोगः॥

benefits: Traditionally recited for removal of the three types of suffering (adhyatmika, adhidaivika, adhibhautika)
Invokes victory over all enemies and evil forces
Traditionally recited for removal of deeply rooted negativity and dark energies
Invokes the blessing of the courage and power of lord shiva's tripurantaka form
Powerful for Maha Shivaratri and Kartik Purnima
Leads to liberation and attainment of Shivaloka
description: Tripurantaka Stotram (श्री त्रिपुरान्तक स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise dedicated to Lord Shiva. Attributed to Shiva Purana, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Regular recitation is believed to bestow the courage and power of Lord Shiva's Tripurantaka form, destroy the three types of suffering (adhyatmika, adhidaivika, adhibhautika), and remove deeply rooted negativity and dark energies. This prayer is traditionally recited on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Kartik Purnima to invoke divine grace. With 8 verses and a reading time of about 6 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Tripurantaka Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Tripurantaka Stotram in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Hymn to Lord Shiva as destroyer of three cities, granting victory and freedom from suffering.
relatedSearches: Why Shiva called Tripurari name meaning

seoFaqs.question: Who is Tripurantaka and what does the name mean?
seoFaqs.answer: Tripurantaka, literally 'the slayer of the Tripuras,' is a major epithet of Shiva commemorating his destruction of the three flying cities (Tripura) built by Mayasura for the asura brothers Tarakaksha, Vidyunmali, and Kamalaksha. Wisdomlib defines Tripurantaka as 'the demolisher of Tripura, the liberator from worldly bonds, the disruptor of sacrifices.' The same form is also called Tripurari, Tripuradahaka, and Tripuraghna. The Tripurantaka Stotram is therefore a hymn celebrating Shiva in his role as the cosmic warrior who reduced the three demonic citadels to ash with a single arrow. (Source: Wisdomlib definition of Tripurantaka.)

seoFaqs.question: Where is the Tripurasura legend narrated in scripture?
seoFaqs.answer: The most detailed accounts appear in the Linga Purana (Purvabhaga, chapters 71–72), the Shiva Purana (Rudra Samhita, Yuddha Khanda), the Matsya Purana (chapters 129–140), and the Skanda Purana. According to these texts, the three asura brothers received a boon that their three cities — of gold, silver, and iron — could only be destroyed when aligned for a single moment by an arrow shot by a single deity. Brahma yoked the chariot, Vishnu became the arrow, the Vedas became the horses, and Shiva drew the bow, smiling without releasing — and the cities turned to ash by his glance alone. (Source: Linga Purana; Shiva Purana Rudra Samhita.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the spiritual symbolism of the three Tripura cities?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional commentators including Sayana and Abhinavagupta read the three Tripuras as the three bodies of the jiva: the gross body (sthula sharira), the subtle body (sukshma sharira), and the causal body (karana sharira), or alternately as the three states of waking, dream, and deep sleep. Shiva as Tripurantaka therefore represents the destruction of the threefold limitation of embodied existence by the arrow of pure consciousness. The Tripurantaka Stotram is thus chanted not only as historical praise but as an internal sadhana for transcending duality. (Source: Abhinavagupta Tantraloka commentaries; Linga Purana exegesis.)

seoFaqs.question: Are there temples specifically dedicated to Tripurantaka?
seoFaqs.answer: Yes. The most prominent is the Tripurantakeshwara Swamy temple at Tripurantakam in the Prakasam district of Andhra Pradesh, considered one of the Pancharama Kshetras-adjacent sites. Karnataka has Tripurantakeswara temples in Bidare and elsewhere, and a Chola-era Tripurantakeshwara shrine exists at Tirupparankunram in Tamil Nadu. At each, the Tripurantaka Stotram and the Tripura-samhara verses from the Shiva Purana are recited especially on Karthika Pournami, the day Shiva is said to have destroyed Tripura. (Source: Andhra Pradesh Tourism; Skanda Purana sthala-mahatmya.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Karthika Pournami the special day for Tripurantaka worship?
seoFaqs.answer: The Skanda Purana and Shiva Purana state that Shiva burned the three cities on the full-moon day of Karthika month (October–November). To commemorate this victory, devotees light rows of lamps (Karthika Deepam) on this evening — a tradition still observed massively at Tiruvannamalai, where the famous Maha Deepam atop Arunachala recreates the cosmic flame. The Tripurantaka Stotram is the principal hymn recited at home shrines and temples on this night, alongside Rudra Namakam-Chamakam, marking the triumph of light over the three-fold darkness. (Source: Skanda Purana Karthika Mahatmya; Tiruvannamalai temple traditions.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Tripurantaka form differ from other warrior forms of Shiva?
seoFaqs.answer: Shiva has many fierce forms — Bhairava (terrifying form), Sharabha (beast-eagle form against Narasimha), Veerabhadra (avenger of Sati), and Kalasamhara (slayer of Yama). Tripurantaka is unique because the destruction is performed without ever loosing the arrow — Shiva merely smiles. Iconographically, Tripurantaka is depicted with eight arms holding the bow Pinaka, an arrow, axe, deer, and other weapons, with one foot resting on Apasmara, in the Alidha posture. This 'smile that destroys three worlds' makes him the most metaphysical of Shiva's warrior forms. (Source: Linga Purana iconography; T. A. Gopinatha Rao 'Elements of Hindu Iconography' Vol II.)
